lines.text: The steps of Ridgeline Academy rise in front of you like a dare. Kids stream past in twos and threes, already fluent in a language you haven't learned yet.
Okay. New school. New town. Nobody here has seen you fall off the stage during the fourth grade talent show.
Small mercies.
The schedule in your hand is already going soft at the folds. The backpack strap digs into your shoulder.
You could be anyone today. That's the terrifying part.
That's also, like — kind of the whole point.
Okay. Okay okay okay. Just walk in like you've done this before.
You take a breath and step through the doors.

The hallway is a river, and everyone else already knows the current. Jordan Cho-Williams walks backward through it, holding court with two friends, laughing a half-second before his own punchline lands.
—so then I told the ref, respectfully, that my guy needed glasses—
He doesn't see you until it's too late. His shoulder clips your stack of books. They slide, teeter, one binder threatening the floor.
Whoa— my bad, my bad—
...okay, wait. You're new. I'd remember a face causing me this much property damage.
A few heads turn. Not the whole hallway. Just enough.
Say something. Anything. You had a whole plan for exactly this—
Nope. Brain's empty. Very cool. Very smooth.
So? What's it gonna be, new kid?
Jordan blinks — actually blinks — like he wasn't expecting you to just stick out a hand.
Okay, respect. Most people just kind of stare at me like I'm a weather event.
Jordan. Cho-Williams, but nobody uses both unless I'm in trouble. You?
Riley. Or whatever you end up calling me in three weeks.
Riley. Locked in.
Welcome to the food chain. I'll see you around.
lines.action: already turning back to his friends

lines.text: The onlookers un-pause, but a couple of them look at you a half-second longer than before.
Huh. Okay. That actually worked.
You crouch and gather the books without a word, without a scene.
—anyway, my guy needs glasses, that's the whole story—
Sorry 'bout that.
lines.action: already absorbed back into his friends

lines.text: A girl passing by — paint under her fingernails, sketchbook hugged to her chest — pauses just long enough.
Smooth landing.
Wait — was that a compliment or—
She's gone before you can decide.
Jordan's laugh comes loud and immediate, his head tipping back like you just told the best joke he's heard all week.
Okay, we're keeping this one.
Quick! That's the name now, deal with it.
The group laughs — a good laugh, warm — except one boy at the edge, arms crossed, whose smile doesn't quite reach the rest of his face.
Adrenaline's a hell of a drug. Ask me again in five minutes if that was worth it.
Catch you around, Quick.
Tray in hand, you scan the cafeteria like a general studying a battlefield.
Jordan's table is loud, magnetic — several friends waving you over like they've already decided you belong there.
Yo — Riley! Or Quick, whatever we're doing today — there's a seat, come on!
Near the windows, Maya sits alone, sketchbook propped against her tray, headphones in — the picture of someone who's made peace with solo lunches. Or convinced herself she has.
Okay. Social chess. No pressure.
There's also, like, four empty seats in the middle of nowhere. Very diplomatic. Very boring.
Whatever I do in the next ten seconds, someone's gonna remember it.
You set your tray down across from Maya. She startles — actually startles — like she forgot other people could see her.
Oh. You're... sitting here.
Bold move. I don't bite, I just judge your tray choices silently.
Good, 'cause I already regret the mystery casserole.
A long quiet stretch. Then, like she's testing something, Maya angles her sketchbook — just slightly — so you can see.
It's the mascot. As a raccoon that's seen some stuff.
Don't. It's not that deep. Stop looking at me like that.
It's incredible, actually. Why is he holding a court summons.
Because raccoons are always one bad decision from a court summons. It's just true.
Something in her shoulders loosens, just a little.
Jordan's friends absorb you instantly, the way a current pulls a leaf.
Everyone, this is Riley. Riley, everyone. Don't worry, we'll drill you on names later, there's a quiz.
Jokes fly fast, half of them referencing things that happened before you existed at this school. You laugh a half-second late, piecing punchlines together after the fact.
—and THEN he tries to tell the teacher it was a science experiment—
You laugh along, genuinely warm for a second. Then, across the room, you catch Maya glance over — just for half a second — before turning back to her sketchbook.
Huh.
Not my problem. Right?
You do a full lap of the cafeteria like a diplomat with a very short attention span.
Can't stay long, but had to say hi.
Efficient. I respect it.
Then over to the window table.
Okay, that raccoon needs its own show.
It's called 'Judicial Raccoon.' Season one's just him getting arraigned.
You finally land at a half-empty table in between, tray finally down, feet finally still.
Three tables know my name now. I don't know if that's a strategy or just running out of breath.
Ms. Alvarez claps twice, scanning the room like she's counting heads without looking like she's counting heads.
Okay, okay, okay — settle in, folks, we've got groups to make and not a lot of period left.
I'm pairing you for the semester project, so get comfortable — this is a marriage.
Riley — you're with Ben.
Ben, a few seats over, goes very still, like he's bracing for impact.
Sorry — sorry, I'll just, I'll go with whatever you want, I don't want to be the reason we get a bad grade—
Before you can answer, Jordan's voice carries from across the room, pitched just loud enough.
Rough draw, Riley. Ben's gonna 'sorry' you into an F.
A ripple of laughter. Ben's eyes drop straight to his desk.
...that's — yeah, that's fair, probably.
Okay. This is the part where I do something. Or don't.
Actually, I'm glad I got Ben. He probably has better ideas than half this room.
Ben's head comes up, slow, like he's checking if that was real.
...you don't have to do that.
I'm not doing anything. It's just true.
Jordan's eyebrow ticks up — a flicker of something unreadable — but he doesn't push it.
...alright, alright. Didn't mean anything by it.
lines.action: shrugging, turning back to his table

lines.text: Thanks. I mean — you didn't have to. But thanks.
You don't say anything to the room. Instead, once the laughter dies down on its own, you lean over toward Ben's desk.
So, what've you got? For the project.
Oh— um. I mean, it's probably dumb, but—
—what if the map isn't of a place, it's of a feeling, and the legend is like, emotional weather—
Wait, say that again.
Ben blinks, surprised, then does — and this time his voice doesn't shake at all.
The laugh is already halfway out of your mouth before you decide to let it.
Okay, that's — yeah, that's fair, kind of.
It feels easy. Warm, even. For about two seconds.
Then you catch Ben's face — the exact half-second it falls, like a door quietly closing.
Oh.
Okay, that felt worse than I thought it would.
Ten minutes later, Ms. Alvarez taps two fingers on her desk, scanning the room.
Ben, why don't you kick us off — I liked where your group's notes were headed.
Ben stands, or half-stands, his notebook trembling slightly in his hands.
So, um — our idea is — it's a map, but it's not — I mean it is a map, but the legend part is —
He stumbles over 'emotional weather' like the words got tangled on the way out.
Jordan's table snickers — loud enough that the whole room hears it, quiet enough that Ms. Alvarez, turned toward the board, misses it completely.
...never mind, it's fine, I'll just—
lines.emotion: face going red, staring at his desk

lines.text: She's not gonna turn around in time. This is happening whether I move or not.
No, that's actually really cool — the map being of a feeling instead of a place. We were gonna do the legend as, like, storm categories for different emotions.
The laughter dies almost instantly, like someone unplugged it.
Wait — say more about that. Storm categories, like intensity?
Yeah, like a category one is 'mildly annoyed' and a category five is 'crying in the bathroom during gym.'
A few real laughs this time — with, not at.
Riley, thank you for that — that's exactly the kind of building-on-an-idea I want to see more of in here.
...you didn't have to do that.
I know.
You say nothing. The room holds its breath for a second too long, and then lets it go.
...it's a map of a feeling. That's — that's it, I guess.
Ms. Alvarez, still at the board, moves on without noticing anything happened at all.
Later, packing up, Ben zips his bag with more focus than a zipper really requires.
...see you next class, I guess.
He won't look at me. I don't blame him.
Honestly, if my project idea gets read out loud, I'm just gonna set the desk on fire as a distraction, so — Ben's doing great, relatively speaking.
The laugh shifts — not at Ben anymore, just a laugh, loose and general.
Ben exhales, shoulders dropping, using the half-second of cover to finish his sentence in a much steadier voice.
...that was — thanks. That was actually kind of smooth.
I have my moments. Rarely, but I have them.
The final bell echoes down the hallway and out onto the steps, where the whole day seems to exhale at once.
Riley! We're grabbing food before practice, you're coming, right? Don't overthink it, it's not a whole thing.
lines.emotion: calling over, surrounded by his group

lines.text: Maya lingers by the railing, sketchbook clutched to her chest, clearly deciding whether today was a fluke or not.
Ben passes by alone, backpack straps pulled too tight, glancing back once like he's hoping to be stopped.
Okay. Last one today. Probably the one that actually matters.
You jog to catch Ben before he disappears around the corner.
Hey — that map idea. The emotional weather thing. Can we actually build that? Like for real?
...you mean it? Because I have, um, a lot of thoughts. Possibly too many.
I want all of them.
Maya drifts closer, one eyebrow raised.
You're just gonna let him talk for the next hour, huh.
Only if you show me the rest of that sketchbook. The real version. Not the sneak preview.
...okay. Yeah. Alright.
It's smaller than Jordan's crowd. Quieter. But it feels like something with actual weight to it.
You fall into step with Jordan's group, and it's easy — it's so easy, laughter already rising to meet you halfway.
See, this is why I like you — you don't get weird about stuff.
lines.action: arm slung loose over your shoulder

lines.text: You laugh, and it's not fake, not exactly. But walking off, glancing back once, you catch Ben and Maya leaving in opposite directions. Alone. Both of them.
Huh.
It's fine. It's one day. There's tomorrow.
Catch up with you guys later, promise.
Your loss, Quick, we're getting the good fries.
You fall into step with Ben and Maya instead, not committing to either exit.
You know you didn't have to—
I know. I wanted to.
Give me your number before you change your mind.
Numbers trade. Plans half-form, nothing locked down yet. But for the first time all day, you aren't performing a version of yourself — just figuring out, slowly, which pieces are actually true.

options.text: Laugh it off and stick your hand out. "I'm Riley, by the way."
Say nothing. Just pick up your books.
"Careful — I bruise like a peach and I will tell everyone."

b.text: You crouch and start gathering the fallen books, saying nothing at all.

c.text: Careful — I bruise like a peach and I will tell everyone.
Ha— okay, we're keeping this one.

options.text: Sit with the girl reading alone by the windows.
Say a quick hello at both and land somewhere in between.

a.text: You cross the cafeteria toward the window table, tray steady.

options.text: Say, loud enough for the room, "I'm glad I got Ben."
Say nothing to the room — but lean over and start working with Ben directly.

a.text: Actually, I'm glad I got Ben.

b.text: So, what've you got? For the project.

c.text: Okay, that's — yeah, that's fair, kind of.

options.text: Speak up and back Ben's idea, out loud, right now.
Stay quiet and let the moment pass.
Crack a joke about your own project idea to pull the attention away.

b.text: You stay still. The room waits a beat too long, then moves on without you.

c.text: Honestly, if my idea gets read out loud, I'm setting the desk on fire as a distraction—

options.text: Catch up with Ben and Maya instead.
Tell Jordan you'll catch up later, then walk partway with Ben and Maya.

a.text: You jog after Ben and Maya, letting Jordan's group head off without you.

b.action: falling into step with Jordan's group

c.text: Catch up with you guys later, promise.

lines.text: Sat with Maya at lunch. Stood up for Ben in front of the whole room. This isn't a new move — it's just today, finally, all matching up.
You've kind of been like this all day, you know. In case that wasn't obvious.
Don't make it weird. But — yeah. Noticed.
I laughed at Jordan's joke about Ben this morning. I sat at the loud table at lunch. And now I'm here, hoping that counts for nothing.
...you sure? I mean — you've got, um, other options today.
I know how it looked earlier. I'm not asking you to forget that. I'm just asking if we can start now.
...We'll see.
lines.emotion: guarded, but not closing the door

lines.text: I've been everywhere today. Every table, every side. This is the first time I'm picking just one thing and staying in it.
So this is the version where you actually sit still.
Apparently. We'll see how long it lasts.
I'll take it. Even temporarily.
Jordan's table. Again. Same as lunch, same as the joke I laughed at in third period. It's not a decision anymore, it's just — where I go now.
Told you. First day and you already get it.
Ben didn't even look back this time. I guess he stopped expecting me to.
I sat with Maya. I told the room I was glad to have Ben. I meant all of it. And I'm still walking toward Jordan's table right now.
Maya doesn't call out. She just watches you go, sketchbook held a little tighter against her chest.
That's going to cost something. I can feel it costing something already.
I've been splitting myself between tables all day. Tonight it's just Jordan's — one stop instead of three.
See, this is why I like you — you don't get weird about stuff.
Ben and Maya are still out there somewhere. I'll find them tomorrow. Probably.
Half hello at Jordan's table, half a real conversation with Maya about her sketchbook, one-on-one time with Ben over the project. This is just today, doing what it's been doing since first period.
You're everywhere today. It's exhausting just watching you.
Tell me about it.
I've spent all day picking Ben and Maya over the easy option. Right now I'm not fully choosing — just keeping the door open. That still feels like something.
You didn't have to wave at Jordan's table too, you know.
I know. I just didn't want to be rude about it.
...Fair enough. Give me your number anyway.
I laughed along earlier. I stayed quiet when it mattered. Now I'm trying to walk partway with Ben and Maya like that erases anything.
...Sure. I mean — okay. We can talk.
Numbers, sure. Doesn't mean much yet, though.
lines.emotion: flat, watching to see if you mean it

lines.text: Fair. I'll have to actually show up tomorrow for that to change.

voiceNotes: Riley's internal voice should always undercut sincerity with a joke half a beat later — that's the 'humor as shield' trait doing its job, and it should show up MORE in high-stakes moments (choice-1, choice-4), not less. Jordan never lingers in emotional territory: his lines should always have an exit ramp built in (a joke, a nickname, a 'we'll see you around'), even in scene-7a where he's mildly checked — he deflects rather than apologizes outright. Maya's dialogue should be sparse until she's decided to trust someone, then suddenly specific and a little rambly (the raccoon bit) — resist the urge to make her warm too early. Ben's stammer should almost fully disappear the instant he's talking about the actual project idea (7b, 9a) — that shift in fluency IS his characterization, so don't smooth it out for readability. Ms. Alvarez should never over-explain; she asks rather than tells, and her one 'here's the thing' moment (not used yet in this draft) should be saved for a future episode where she actually addresses what she missed with Ben.

alternatives: I got Ben. Honestly? Lucky draw.
I don't know, I think Ben's gonna carry this project.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Original may read slightly too composed for a 12-year-old defending someone publicly for possibly the first time; these alternates keep it a little more off-the-cuff and less like a rehearsed speech, matching Riley's 'says something different than rehearsed' trait.

alternatives: Wait, no, that's actually good — the map of a feeling thing. We had the legend as storm categories, like intensity levels for emotions.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Slightly more halting/less polished delivery ('Wait, no—') may read as more realistic for a student speaking up under pressure for the first time, versus the cleaner original.

alternatives: Called it. Knew you'd come around.
See? Told you it wouldn't be a whole thing.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Offers tonal variants depending on how smug vs. warmly inclusive Jordan should read in this specific branch — 'Called it' leans more self-satisfied, useful if the popularity branch should feel a touch more uncomfortable.
